skill.name: ai-automation-workflows
skill.description: Build automated AI workflows with inference.sh, shell scripts, scheduled jobs, batch processing, retry logic, logging, and Python wrappers.
skill.summary: Create repeatable AI automation workflows for content, data, monitoring, and scheduled generation.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly true positives for risky shell automation guidance inside Markdown examples. I found no prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent, but the pipe-to-shell installer, cron persistence guidance, webhook posting, and repeated command substitutions make this unsafe to publish without changes.

high_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download an install script over HTTPS and pipe it directly into a shell before login. This is a true positive because it executes remote code without requiring local inspection first.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present in the quick start. The install note reduces malicious intent concerns, but it does not remove the execution risk.

high_findings.title: Cron Persistence Guidance
high_findings.description: The scheduled automation section tells users to edit crontab and add recurring AI workflow jobs. This is a true positive because cron entries create persistence and can repeatedly run scripts that call external services.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The crontab edit instruction and recurring schedule are explicit. The context is legitimate automation, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Repeated Shell Command Substitution
medium_findings.description: Multiple workflow examples embed command output, variables, and user-provided text into JSON payloads for external AI calls. This can cause injection, quoting, privacy, and data handling issues when adapted without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitutions and variable interpolation are directly shown in shell examples. They are tutorial patterns, but they are risky when copied into production.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Alert May Expose Command Output
medium_findings.description: The monitoring example posts error text and command details to a webhook endpoint. This is a true positive for network exposure risk because failures can include sensitive prompts, paths, or service responses.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example sends captured command output and command arguments to a remote endpoint. The endpoint is a placeholder, so the intent appears instructional rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Python Wrapper Executes External CLI
medium_findings.description: The Python SDK example calls the inference.sh CLI with subprocess.run. The argument-list form avoids shell injection, but it still runs external commands and processes remote AI results.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is explicit and should be documented as external command execution. Risk is moderated because shell=True is not used and the arguments are structured.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings do not correspond to weak cryptographic operations in the reviewed context. The visible checksum reference is SHA-256 verification, which is not a weak algorithm in this use case.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata, date substitution, and best-practice text rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or similar weak cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Service References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the skill image, inference.sh installation resources, and a placeholder webhook example. These are network risk indicators, but they are not evidence of hidden exfiltration by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and consistent with installation, documentation, media, or placeholder alerting. They still warrant user review because workflows send data to external services.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: A curl command pipes a remote installer directly into sh. This pattern should be replaced with manual download, verification, and explicit execution steps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present. It is one of the highest-risk patterns in the file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Scheduled Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Cron examples create recurring execution of AI automation scripts. This can be legitimate, but it also creates persistence and recurring external network activity.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The crontab command and recurring schedule are explicit. The example lacks safety controls such as least privilege, review, and bounded runtime.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Expansion Into External Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples embed command output and file content into prompts sent to external model services. This can leak local data or break request structure if inputs are not sanitized.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples show shell substitution in request payloads. The intent is workflow automation, but production copies need input validation and privacy controls.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable AI workflows that can run on a schedule or process many inputs. This skill provides script patterns for inference.sh automation, batching, retries, logging, alerts, and Python wrappers.
actual_capabilities: Shows bash patterns for batch, sequential, parallel, conditional, and retry workflows.
Provides cron examples for scheduled AI content generation and recurring reports.
Demonstrates logging and alerting wrappers for command-line AI workflows.
Includes a Python subprocess wrapper for calling the inference.sh CLI.
Documents templates for content calendars and data processing pipelines.
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI access and configured authentication.
Examples may need security review before production use.
Does not provide a complete deployment framework or managed scheduler.
Does not validate sensitive data before sending prompts to external services.

use_cases.title: Schedule Daily Content Generation
use_cases.description: Create recurring jobs that generate images, captions, tips, or weekly reports with inference.sh apps.

use_cases.title: Process Data Files in Batches
use_cases.description: Run the same AI analysis workflow across multiple text files and save separate outputs for review.

use_cases.title: Build Monitored AI Pipelines
use_cases.description: Combine retries, fallbacks, logs, and alerts so automated AI scripts are easier to operate.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Batch Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user automating repeated image or text generation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a small batch workflow for these inputs: [list inputs]. Include rate limits, output file names, and clear setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Scheduled Content Job
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a recurring content workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a scheduled AI content workflow for [content type]. Include the schedule, script outline, output folders, logging, and review checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Add Reliability Controls
prompt_templates.scenario: User has an existing workflow and needs safer operation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this AI workflow and add retries, fallback behavior, logging, and alerting. Explain each operational safeguard in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Step Automation Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user orchestrating several AI services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a multi-step inference.sh pipeline for [goal]. Include batch inputs, parallel steps, error handling, data privacy checks, and deployment considerations.

output_examples.input: Generate a workflow for five weekly social media posts.
output_examples.output: A plan with topic selection, image generation, caption generation, output folders, rate limits, and a manual approval step.

output_examples.input: Add monitoring to an AI image generation script.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline that records start time, success status, errors, duration, and a safe alert message without sensitive prompt text.

output_examples.input: Convert a one-off AI command into a production batch job.
output_examples.output: A structured process with input validation, retry rules, output naming, logging, and a final review checklist.

best_practices: Review every generated script before running it, especially install, cron, and webhook commands.
Keep sensitive data out of prompts, logs, filenames, and alert payloads.
Use small batches, rate limits, retries, and clear output folders for repeatable operation.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers directly into a shell without manual verification.
Do not schedule automation jobs before testing them interactively with safe sample data.
Do not send raw local files, command output, or secrets to external services without approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps design inference.sh workflows for batch processing, scheduled jobs, content generation, monitoring, and data analysis.

faq.question: Does it require inference.sh?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples assume the inference.sh CLI is installed, authenticated, and allowed to call selected apps.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to run the examples directly?
faq.answer: No. Review and adapt the examples first, especially commands that install software, edit cron, or send webhook alerts.

faq.question: Can it build a complete production scheduler?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow patterns and scripts, but production scheduling needs access control, monitoring, testing, and incident procedures.

faq.question: How should I protect sensitive data?
faq.answer: Filter inputs, avoid secrets in prompts and logs, redact alert payloads, and confirm which external services receive data.
